museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: June gives Sega's Columns a Mega Drive puzzle identity before the autumn hardware storm.
museum.curatorNote: June has a source-cited Sega software anchor.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Columns is released for Mega Drive in Japan
items.body: Sega's falling-jewel puzzle gives the Mega Drive its own answer to the puzzle-game mood of the era.
items.sourceNote: Japanese catalogue sources cite June 30, 1990.

items.title: Puzzle games remain system language
items.body: After Tetris, every platform wants a clean, repeatable puzzle identity.
items.sourceNote: Tetris/Columns context.

items.title: The European Mega Drive launch is still ahead
items.body: British players are about to meet Sega's 16-bit console properly.
items.sourceNote: European launch follows later in 1990.

items.title: Game Boy anticipation builds in Europe
items.body: Nintendo's handheld is now close enough to feel like a real retail object.
items.sourceNote: European Game Boy launch follows in September.

items.title: No second June anchor is forced
items.body: The drawer keeps Columns central.
items.sourceNote: No other strong June-specific anchor used.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors where the evidence supports them.

items.title: Loom
items.body: Lucasfilm's musical fantasy adventure uses drafts instead of inventory clutter.
items.sourceNote: Giant Bomb and other summaries cite January 1990.

items.title: Smash TV
items.body: Williams turns twin-stick shooting into a violent game-show satire.
items.sourceNote: Arcade-History and other sources cite April 1990.

items.title: Fire Emblem: Shadow Dragon and the Blade of Light
items.body: Intelligent Systems begins Nintendo's tactical RPG lineage.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo's Fire Emblem history cites April 20, 1990.

items.title: Metal Gear 2: Solid Snake
items.body: Kojima's stealth sequel deepens systems, story and surveillance.
items.sourceNote: Sources cite July 20, 1990 Japanese MSX2 release.

items.title: Dr. Mario
items.body: Nintendo gives Mario a puzzle coat in a post-Tetris handheld world.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo Life and other references cite July 27, 1990.

items.title: Wing Commander
items.body: Origin makes PC space combat feel cinematic and character-led.
items.sourceNote: Sources cite September 26, 1990.

items.title: Mega Man 3
items.body: Capcom continues its NES/Famicom action formula as the old machine peaks.
items.sourceNote: Common references cite September 28, 1990.

items.title: The Secret of Monkey Island
items.body: Lucasfilm's pirate comedy turns point-and-click adventure into a warm social memory.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot and other summaries cite October 1990.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four machines or technology contexts that explain the month's place in gaming history.

items.title: Super Famicom
items.body: Nintendo launches its 16-bit console in Japan with Super Mario World and F-Zero.
items.sourceNote: Japanese launch date widely cited.

items.title: Game Boy in Europe
items.body: Nintendo's handheld finally reaches European shops in September.
items.sourceNote: Europe launch date cited as September 28, 1990.

items.title: Game Gear
items.body: Sega launches a colour handheld in Japan, trading battery life for screen spectacle.
items.sourceNote: Sega Retro cites October 6, 1990.

items.title: Amstrad GX4000 and Mega Drive Europe
items.body: Britain's console shelf gets crowded, with Sega's 16-bit machine and Amstrad's late 8-bit cartridge gamble.
items.sourceNote: Mega Drive Europe and GX4000 both arrive in 1990.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Before online gaming was ordinary, paper, shops and local conversations carried the culture.

items.title: Shareware and BBSs become more important
items.body: Commander Keen shows how PC games can spread through uploads, mail order and word of mouth.

items.title: Print still dominates ordinary discovery
items.body: Mean Machines, Zero, ACE and The Games Machine remain the practical browsing interface.

items.title: Regional gaps are social knowledge
items.body: Friends know which machines are out in Japan, America or Britain even when nobody nearby owns them.

items.title: PC gaming has a different route
items.body: DOS games, shareware and hardware requirements make PC culture feel separate from console and microcomputer play.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month as a player might have met it.

items.title: Everything arrived at once
items.body: June 1990 could mean a Game Boy in a UK shop, a Mega Drive advert, a PC adventure, and a Japanese Super Famicom screenshot in the same mental drawer.

items.title: The bedroom was crowded
items.body: Old 8-bit machines, 16-bit computers, handhelds and new consoles all fought for money and attention.

items.title: PC games gained confidence
items.body: Wing Commander, Monkey Island and Commander Keen made DOS feel less like an office machine and more like a platform.

items.title: Britain stood between generations
items.body: The Mega Drive and Game Boy were real; the Super Famicom was a glowing promise from Japan.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for June 1990.
Month-specific dates are used only where the reviewed sources support them; uncertain items are marked in sourceNote/certainty fields.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
